subtopic: Simplifying surd expressions
question: Evaluate $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{12}+\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{12}-\sqrt{3}}\right)^2$
technique: $\sqrt{12}=2\sqrt3$, so $\dfrac{\sqrt{12}+\sqrt3}{\sqrt{12}-\sqrt3}=\dfrac{2\sqrt3+\sqrt3}{2\sqrt3-\sqrt3}=\dfrac{3\sqrt3}{\sqrt3}=3$. Squaring gives $9$.

subtopic: Simultaneous equations word problem
question:
A fruit stall sells apples costing £x each, and pears costing £y each.
Sam bought 2 apples and 5 pears, and the total cost of these was £P.
Lesley bought 3 apples and 2 pears, and the total cost of these was £Q.
Which of the following is an expression for the cost, in pounds (£), of a pear?
technique: $2x+5y=P$, $3x+2y=Q$. Multiply first by 3 and second by 2: $6x+15y=3P$, $6x+4y=2Q$. Subtracting: $11y=3P-2Q$, so $y=\dfrac{3P-2Q}{11}$.

steps.title: Multiply first by and second by
steps.content: Multiply first by 3 and second by 2: $6x+15y=3P$, $6x+4y=2Q$.

subtopic: Combined direct and inverse proportion
question:
P is directly proportional to Q squared.
When P is 2, Q is 4.
Q is inversely proportional to R.
When Q is 2, R is 5.
What is P in terms of R?
technique: $P=kQ^2$: $2=k(16)\Rightarrow k=\frac18$, so $P=\frac{Q^2}{8}$. $Q=\frac{c}{R}$: $2=\frac{c}{5}\Rightarrow c=10$, so $Q=\frac{10}{R}$. Then $P=\dfrac{(10/R)^2}{8}=\dfrac{100}{8R^2}=\dfrac{25}{2R^2}$.

subtopic: Linear word-problem system, three groups
question:
There are 100 students in Year 10.
Each student studies exactly one of French, German, and Spanish.
X girls study French and there are 3X girls in total.
2Y boys study German.
There are 35 students studying Spanish of which Y are boys.
Which of the following is an expression for the total number of students studying German?
technique: Total girls $=3X$; girls doing French $=X$; girls doing Spanish $=35-Y$. Girls doing German $=3X-X-(35-Y)=2X+Y-35$. Total German $=$ German girls $+$ German boys $=(2X+Y-35)+2Y=2X+3Y-35$.

steps.title: Total girls girls doing French girls doing Spanish
steps.content: Total girls $=3X$; girls doing French $=X$; girls doing Spanish $=35-Y$.

steps.title: Total German German girls German boys
steps.content: Total German $=$ German girls $+$ German boys $=(2X+Y-35)+2Y=2X+3Y-35$.

subtopic: Exterior angles of regular polygons
question:
An exterior angle of a regular polygon with n sides is 4° larger than an exterior angle of a regular polygon with (n + 3) sides.
What is the value of n?
technique: Exterior angle of $n$-gon is $\frac{360}{n}$. So $\frac{360}{n}-\frac{360}{n+3}=4$. This gives $\frac{360\times3}{n(n+3)}=4\Rightarrow n(n+3)=270\Rightarrow n^2+3n-270=0\Rightarrow (n-15)(n+18)=0\Rightarrow n=15$.

subtopic: Bearings and isosceles triangles
question:
The bearing of a ship R from a lighthouse L is 220°.
A canoe C is due North of R.
C is the same distance from the ship and the lighthouse.
What is the bearing of L from C?
technique: Bearing of L from R $=220°-180°=40°$. Since C is due north of R, the angle LRC between RC (north) and RL is $40°$. As LC $=$ RC, triangle LRC is isosceles, so angle RLC $=$ angle LRC $=40°$, giving angle RCL $=180°-40°-40°=100°$. Since RC points due south from C, the bearing of L from C is $180°-100°=080°$.

steps.title: Since C is due north of R the
steps.content: Since C is due north of R, the angle LRC between RC (north) and RL is $40^\circ$.

steps.title: As LC RC triangle LRC is isosceles so
steps.content: As LC $=$ RC, triangle LRC is isosceles, so angle RLC $=$ angle LRC $=40^\circ$, giving angle RCL $=180^\circ-40^\circ-40^\circ=100^\circ$.

steps.title: Since RC points due south from C the
steps.content: Since RC points due south from C, the bearing of L from C is $180^\circ-100^\circ=080^\circ$.

subtopic: Clock hand angles
question:
The hands of a 12-hour analogue clock move continuously. When the time on the clock is 4:00, the angle between the minute hand and the hour hand is 120°.
What is the angle between the two hands at 4:40?
technique: At 4:00 the hour hand is at $120°$ from 12. In 40 minutes the hour hand moves $0.5°\times40=20°$, reaching $140°$. The minute hand at 40 minutes is at $40\times6°=240°$. The angle between them is $|240-140|=100°$.

steps.title: At the hour hand is at from
steps.content: At 4:00 the hour hand is at $120^\circ$ from 12.

steps.title: In minutes the hour hand moves reaching
steps.content: In 40 minutes the hour hand moves $0.5^\circ\times40=20^\circ$, reaching $140^\circ$.

steps.title: The minute hand at minutes is at
steps.content: The minute hand at 40 minutes is at $40\times6^\circ=240^\circ$.

subtopic: Probability without replacement
question:
A pet shop has 4 female rabbits and x male rabbits for sale.
A customer buys 2 of the rabbits, chosen at random, and each rabbit is equally likely to be chosen.
The probability that both the chosen rabbits are male is $\frac13$.
What is the value of x?
technique: $P(\text{both male})=\dfrac{x}{x+4}\cdot\dfrac{x-1}{x+3}=\dfrac13$. So $3x(x-1)=(x+4)(x+3)\Rightarrow 3x^2-3x=x^2+7x+12\Rightarrow 2x^2-10x-12=0\Rightarrow x^2-5x-6=0\Rightarrow (x-6)(x+1)=0$, so $x=6$.

subtopic: Area of a square with inscribed semicircle
question:
[DIAGRAM: A square of side length x cm. A circle is drawn with centre O at the mid-point of one side of the square; this side forms part of a diameter of the circle. The circle passes through the two far corners of the square.]
What is the area, in cm², of the shaded part of the semi-circle?
technique: The circle has radius $r=\sqrt{x^2+(x/2)^2}=\dfrac{x\sqrt5}{2}$, so semicircle area $=\dfrac{\pi r^2}{2}=\dfrac{5\pi x^2}{8}$. Subtracting the triangular/square overlap region (area $x^2$) leaves the shaded segment area $\left(\dfrac{5\pi-8}{8}\right)x^2$.

steps.title: The circle has radius so semicircle area
steps.content: The circle has radius $r=\sqrt{x^2+(x/2)^2}=\dfrac{x\sqrt{5}}{2}$, so semicircle area $=\dfrac{\pi r^2}{2}=\dfrac{5\pi x^2}{8}$.

steps.title: Subtracting the triangular square overlap region area leaves
steps.content: Subtracting the triangular/square overlap region (area $x^2$) leaves the shaded segment area $\left(\dfrac{5\pi-8}{8}\right)x^2$.

subtopic: Volume, density and mass of a hollow cylinder
question:
A cylindrical hollow metal pipe is 16 cm long.
It has an external diameter of 10 cm and an internal diameter of 8 cm.
The density of the metal from which the pipe is made is 8 grams per cm³.
[DIAGRAM: hollow cylindrical pipe, length 16 cm, external diameter 10 cm, internal diameter 8 cm, diagram not to scale]
What is the mass of the pipe in grams?
technique: External radius $=5$, internal radius $=4$. Volume $=\pi(5^2-4^2)\times16=\pi\times9\times16=144\pi\text{ cm}^3$. Mass $=144\pi\times8=1152\pi$ g.

subtopic: Distance from a velocity-time graph
question:
A car is travelling along a horizontal road in a straight line.
The graph is a velocity–time graph for part of the car's journey.
[DIAGRAM: velocity (m/s) vs time (s) graph for 0-150s, showing the car accelerating, travelling at constant speed, and decelerating during different segments]
During this part of the journey, what is the total distance that the car travels while it is decelerating?
technique: The distance travelled during deceleration is the area under the velocity-time graph over the deceleration interval (a triangle/trapezium region). Reading the graph values gives a distance of $500\text{ m}$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: The distance travelled during deceleration is the area
steps.content: The distance travelled during deceleration is the area under the velocity-time graph over the deceleration interval (a triangle/ Reading the graph values gives a distance of $500\text{ m}$.

subtopic: Convection and density changes
question:
When a saucepan of water is heated from below, convection currents form and transfer heat through the liquid.
Here are three statements about the water as it is heated:
1 The mass of a fixed volume of the water increases.
2 The density of a fixed mass of the water decreases.
3 The volume of a fixed mass of the water increases.
Which of these statements help(s) to explain how convection currents are formed?
technique: Heating water causes thermal expansion: a fixed mass occupies a larger volume (statement 3 true) and so has lower density (statement 2 true). Statement 1 is false — mass of a fixed volume actually decreases as it expands. Lower density water rises, driving convection, so statements 2 and 3 explain it.

steps.title: Heating water causes thermal expansion a fixed mass
steps.content: Heating water causes thermal expansion: a fixed mass occupies a larger volume (statement 3 true) and so has lower density (statement 2 true).

steps.title: Statement is false mass of a fixed volume
steps.content: Statement 1 is false — mass of a fixed volume actually decreases as it expands.

steps.title: Lower density water rises driving convection so statements
steps.content: Lower density water rises, driving convection, so statements 2 and 3 explain it.

subtopic: Energy efficiency of a motor lifting a load
question:
An electric motor is used to pull a broken-down car slowly from the road up a ramp on to the back of a breakdown truck.
The car has a mass of 1200 kg and is lifted through a vertical height of 1.0 m.
The total input energy to the motor is 28 kJ and it is 75% efficient.
In the process of lifting the car, energy is lost to the surroundings from the motor and from other causes.
[DIAGRAM: motor pulling car up a 1.0 m ramp onto a breakdown truck]
What is the total energy lost to the surroundings?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: Useful output of motor $=0.75\times28=21\text{ kJ}$. Gravitational PE gained by car $=mgh=1200\times10\times1.0=12{,}000\text{ J}=12\text{ kJ}$. Total energy lost to surroundings $=$ total input $-$ PE gained $=28-12=16\text{ kJ}$.

steps.title: Gravitational PE gained by car
steps.content: Gravitational PE gained by car $=mgh=1200\times10\times1.0=12{,}000\text{ J}=12\text{ kJ}$.

steps.title: Total energy lost to surroundings total input PE
steps.content: Total energy lost to surroundings $=$ total input $-$ PE gained $=28-12=16\text{ kJ}$.

subtopic: Gamma-ray timing in a medical scanner
question:
In one type of medical scanner a source is placed inside a patient's body. This source causes pairs of gamma-rays to be emitted simultaneously in opposite directions.
Detectors on each side of the patient are used to detect the gamma-rays. The distance between the two detectors is 3.0 m. When the source is at Q, half-way between the detectors, the two gamma-rays arrive at the same time.
In a particular scan the gamma-rays arrive at the two detectors with a time difference of 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ s.
Assume that, inside the patient, the gamma-rays travel at a speed of 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
How far from Q, half-way between the detectors, is the gamma-ray source?
technique: If the source is a distance $d$ from Q, the path difference between the two gamma-rays is $2d$. Time difference $\Delta t=\dfrac{2d}{c}\Rightarrow d=\dfrac{\Delta t\, c}{2}=\dfrac{4.0\times10^{-10}\times3.0\times10^8}{2}=0.06\text{ m}=6.0\text{ cm}$.

steps.title: If the source is a distance from Q
steps.content: If the source is a distance $d$ from Q, the path difference between the two gamma-rays is $2d$.

steps.title: Simplify to the result
steps.content: Time difference $\Delta t=\dfrac{2d}{c}\Rightarrow d=\dfrac{\Delta t\, c}{2}=\dfrac{4.0\times10^{-10}\times3.0\times10^8}{2}=0.06\text{ m}=6.0\text{ cm}$.

subtopic: Nuclear fission equation balancing
question:
When a plutonium-239 nucleus absorbs a neutron it undergoes nuclear fission. One particular fission reaction results in the creation of xenon and zirconium as daughter nuclei. The nuclear equation for this reaction is shown but with some non-zero integers replaced by the letters w, x, y and z:
$^{239}_{94}\text{Pu} + {}^{1}_{0}n \rightarrow {}^{y}_{z}\text{Zr} + {}^{x}_{54}\text{Xe} + w\,{}^{1}_{0}n$
Which equation is correct?
technique: Mass number conservation: $239+1=x+y+w\Rightarrow 240=x+y+w$. Charge (atomic number) conservation: $94=54+z\Rightarrow z=40$. Rearranging the mass equation for $z$'s complementary relation as given in the options, $z=240-(w+y)$ is the equation consistent with the mass balance $x+y+w=240$ combined with the charge relation.

steps.title: Rearranging the mass equation for s complementary relation
steps.content: Rearranging the mass equation for $z$'s complementary relation as given in the options, $z=240-(w+y)$ is the equation consistent with the mass balance $x+y+w=240$ combined with the charge relation.

subtopic: Motor current from mechanical power and efficiency
question:
An electric motor is connected to a constant 12V d.c. supply. The motor is used to lift a mass of 20 kg by means of a rope and pulley. The mass is lifted vertically through a height of 6.0 m in a time of 5.0s. The complete lifting system (motor, rope and pulley) is 80% efficient.
[DIAGRAM: motor connected via rope and pulley lifting a 20 kg mass through 6.0 m]
What is the current in the electric motor?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: Useful work $=mgh=20\times10\times6=1200\text{ J}$. Since system is 80% efficient, electrical energy input $=\dfrac{1200}{0.8}=1500\text{ J}$. Power $=\dfrac{1500}{5.0}=300\text{ W}$. Current $I=\dfrac{P}{V}=\dfrac{300}{12}=25\text{ A}$.

steps.title: Since system is efficient electrical energy input
steps.content: Since system is 80% efficient, electrical energy input $=\dfrac{1200}{0.8}=1500\text{ J}$.

subtopic: Alpha and beta decay chain identification
question:
The nuclide $^{P}_{Q}X$ decays to the stable nuclide Y. During this process four particles are emitted: an α-particle and three β⁻ particles.
Which of the following is not a nuclide that could be formed at any stage during this process?
(Table gives atomic mass and atomic number relative to P and Q)

options.A: atomic mass P, atomic number Q − 1
options.B: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 1
options.C: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 2
options.D: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 3
options.E: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q − 2
options.F: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q − 1
options.G: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q
options.H: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q + 1

technique: Alpha decay changes mass by $-4$ and atomic number by $-2$; each beta minus decay leaves mass unchanged and increases atomic number by $+1$. Depending on the order of the 4 emissions, reachable intermediate states are: mass $P$ with atomic number $Q, Q+1, Q+2, Q+3$ (before the alpha is emitted), or mass $P-4$ with atomic number $Q-2, Q-1, Q, Q+1$ (after the alpha is emitted). The combination of atomic number $Q-1$ with mass still $P$ is not reachable, since before the alpha the atomic number can only increase from $Q$.

steps.title: Alpha decay changes mass by and atomic number
steps.content: Alpha decay changes mass by $-4$ and atomic number by $-2$; each beta minus decay leaves mass unchanged and increases atomic number by $+1$.

steps.title: Depending on the order of the emissions reachable
steps.content: Depending on the order of the 4 emissions, reachable intermediate states are: mass $P$ with atomic number $Q, Q+1, Q+2, Q+3$ (before the alpha is emitted), or mass $P-4$ with atomic number $Q-2, Q-1, Q, Q+1$ (after the alpha is emitted).

steps.title: The combination of atomic number with mass still
steps.content: The combination of atomic number $Q-1$ with mass still $P$ is not reachable, since before the alpha the atomic number can only increase from $Q$.

subtopic: Atomic vs nuclear density ratio
question:
The radius of an iron-56 atom is 3.0 × 10⁴ times greater than the radius of an iron-56 nucleus.
What is the value of $\dfrac{\text{density of an iron atom}}{\text{density of an iron nucleus}}$?
technique: Since almost all the atom's mass is in the nucleus, the mass is roughly equal for atom and nucleus. Volume scales as radius cubed, so volume ratio (atom:nucleus) is $(3.0\times10^4)^3$. Density is inversely proportional to volume for equal mass, so the density ratio is $(3.0\times10^4)^{-3}$.

steps.title: Since almost all the atom s mass is
steps.content: Since almost all the atom's mass is in the nucleus, the mass is roughly equal for atom and nucleus.

steps.title: Volume scales as radius cubed so volume ratio
steps.content: Volume scales as radius cubed, so volume ratio (atom:nucleus) is $(3.0\times10^4)^3$.

steps.title: Density is inversely proportional to volume for equal
steps.content: Density is inversely proportional to volume for equal mass, so the density ratio is $(3.0\times10^4)^{-3}$.

subtopic: Wave speed from displacement graphs
question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph 1 shows displacement vs time for one particle of a medium as a wave passes, with marked times t1 and t2. Graph 2 shows displacement vs distance for the same wave at one instant, with marked positions x1 and x2.]
Which expression gives the speed in m s⁻¹ of the wave?
technique: Wave speed $=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$. Reading the fractional periods/wavelengths represented by $(t_2-t_1)$ and $(x_2-x_1)$ on the two graphs gives speed $=\dfrac{3(x_2-x_1)}{t_2-t_1}$.

steps.title: Reading the fractional periods wavelengths represented by
steps.content: Reading the fractional periods/wavelengths represented by $(t_2-t_1)$ and $(x_2-x_1)$ on the two graphs gives speed $=\dfrac{3(x_2-x_1)}{t_2-t_1}$.

subtopic: Charge from power, voltage and time
question:
A kettle is designed to work from a car's power socket. The kettle has a power rating of 150 W when a constant voltage of 12.0V d.c. is applied across its element.
How much charge passes through the element of this kettle when the voltage of 12.0V is applied across it for 20 minutes?
technique: Current $I=\dfrac{P}{V}=\dfrac{150}{12}=12.5\text{ A}$. Time $t=20\times60=1200\text{ s}$. Charge $Q=It=12.5\times1200=15{,}000\text{ C}$.

subtopic: Momentum conservation in a train collision
question:
A freight train travelling on a straight horizontal track at 2.0 m s⁻¹ collides with a passenger train travelling at 5.0 m s⁻¹ in the opposite direction. Both trains immediately come to a complete stop on the track.
The freight train has three locomotives of 130 tonnes each and seven container wagons of 30 tonnes each. The passenger train has two locomotives of 70 tonnes each and a number of passenger carriages of 10 tonnes each.
How many passenger carriages does the passenger train have?
technique: Freight train mass $=3(130)+7(30)=390+210=600$ tonnes, momentum $=600\times2.0=1200$ (tonne·m/s). For the system to stop completely, total momentum $=0$: passenger train mass $\times5.0=1200\Rightarrow$ mass $=240$ tonnes. Passenger train mass $=2(70)+10n=140+10n=240\Rightarrow n=10$ carriages.

steps.title: Freight train mass tonnes momentum tonne m s
For the system to stop completely total momentum
steps.content: For the system to stop completely, total momentum $=0$: passenger train mass $\times5.0=1200\Rightarrow$ mass $=240$ tonnes.

subtopic: Terminal velocity: energy and forces
question:
Consider the following three statements about a parachutist of mass 72 kg falling vertically at a constant velocity of 5.0 m s⁻¹ after the parachute has opened:
1 The parachutist has a constant kinetic energy of 1800 J.
2 The parachutist is losing gravitational potential energy at a rate of 3600 J s⁻¹.
3 Air resistance and the force of gravity acting on the parachutist are a Newton's third law pair of forces.
Which of the statements is/are correct?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: $KE=\frac12mv^2=\frac12(72)(5^2)=900\text{ J}$, not 1800 J, so statement 1 is false. Rate of loss of PE $=mgv=72\times10\times5=3600\text{ J/s}$, so statement 2 is true. Air resistance and gravity act on the *same* object (the parachutist), so they cannot be a Newton's third law pair (which requires forces on two different objects); statement 3 is false. Only statement 2 is correct.

steps.title: Not J so statement is false
steps.content: $KE=\frac12mv^2=\frac12(72)(5^2)=900\text{ J}$, not 1800 J, so statement 1 is false.

steps.title: Rate of loss of PE so statement is
steps.content: Rate of loss of PE $=mgv=72\times10\times5=3600\text{ J/s}$, so statement 2 is true.

steps.title: Air resistance and gravity act on the same
steps.content: Air resistance and gravity act on the *same* object (the parachutist), so they cannot be a Newton's third law pair (which requires forces on two different objects); statement 3 is false.

subtopic: Comparing half-lives of two decaying sources
question:
Two radioactive sources X and Y have half-lives of 3.0 hours and 2.0 hours respectively. The product of the decay of both of the sources is a stable isotope of the element Z.
Six hours ago a mixture contained the same number of atoms of both X and Y, and no other atoms.
What fraction of the mixture is now made up of atoms of Z?
technique: Let initial atoms of each $=N$. After 6h ($=2$ half-lives of X), X remaining $=N/4$, decayed $=3N/4$. After 6h ($=3$ half-lives of Y), Y remaining $=N/8$, decayed $=7N/8$. Total Z $=3N/4+7N/8=13N/8$. Total atoms now $=N/4+N/8+13N/8=2N$. Fraction Z $=\dfrac{13N/8}{2N}=\dfrac{13}{16}$.

steps.title: After h half lives of X X remaining
steps.content: After 6h ($=2$ half-lives of X), X remaining $=N/4$, decayed $=3N/4$.

steps.title: After h half lives of Y Y remaining
steps.content: After 6h ($=3$ half-lives of Y), Y remaining $=N/8$, decayed $=7N/8$.

subtopic: Comparing kinetic energies via mass and speed ratios
question:
Car X passes car Y on a motorway.
Car X is travelling at 1.5 times the speed of car Y.
The mass of car X is $\frac45$ of the mass of car Y.
How do the kinetic energies of the two cars compare?

options.A: kinetic energy of car X = 0.90 × kinetic energy of car Y
options.B: kinetic energy of car X = 0.96 × kinetic energy of car Y
options.C: kinetic energy of car X = 1.20 × kinetic energy of car Y
options.D: kinetic energy of car X = 1.44 × kinetic energy of car Y
options.E: kinetic energy of car X = 1.80 × kinetic energy of car Y

technique: $\dfrac{KE_X}{KE_Y}=\dfrac{m_X v_X^2}{m_Y v_Y^2}=\dfrac45\times1.5^2=\dfrac45\times2.25=1.8$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Solving a quadratic inequality
question: Solve fully the inequality $2x^2+x-15\ge0$
technique: Factorise: $2x^2+x-15=(x+3)(2x-5)$. Roots at $x=-3$ and $x=2.5$. Since the parabola opens upward, $2x^2+x-15\ge0$ outside the roots: $x\le-3$ or $x\ge2.5$.

steps.title: Since the parabola opens upward outside the roots
steps.content: Since the parabola opens upward, $2x^2+x-15\ge0$ outside the roots: $x\le-3$ or $x\ge2.5$.

subtopic: Rearranging a quadratic equation for x
question:
The equation gives $y$ in terms of $x$:
$y=3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}-1\right)^2-5$
Which one of the following is a rearrangement for $x$ in terms of $y$?
technique: $y=3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}-1\right)^2-5 \Rightarrow y+5=3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}-1\right)^2 \Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{x}{2}-1\right)^2=\dfrac{y+5}{3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{x}{2}-1=\pm\sqrt{\dfrac{y+5}{3}} \Rightarrow x=2\pm2\sqrt{\dfrac{y+5}{3}}$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Simplifying an algebraic fraction
question: Which one of the following is a simplification of $2-\dfrac{x^2(9x^2-4)}{x^3(2-3x)}$?

subtopic: solving exponential equations with indices
question:
What is the value of $x$ that makes the following expression correct?
$2^{3+2x}\,4^x\,8^{-x}=4\sqrt2$
technique: Express all terms as powers of 2: $4^x=2^{2x}$, $8^{-x}=2^{-3x}$, and $4\sqrt2=2^{2.5}$. The left side becomes $2^{(3+2x)+2x-3x}=2^{3+x}$. Setting exponents equal: $3+x=2.5$, so $x=-0.5$.

steps.title: Express all terms as powers of and
steps.content: Express all terms as powers of 2: $4^x=2^{2x}$, $8^{-x}=2^{-3x}$, and $4\sqrt{2}=2^{2.5}$.

subtopic: Simplifying nested surd/fraction expression
question: Which one of the following is a simplification of $1-\left(\dfrac{3+\sqrt3}{6-2\sqrt3}\right)^2$?
technique: $\dfrac{3+\sqrt3}{6-2\sqrt3}=\dfrac{3+\sqrt3}{2(3-\sqrt3)}=\dfrac{(3+\sqrt3)^2}{2(9-3)}=\dfrac{12+6\sqrt3}{12}=1+\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$. Squaring: $1+\sqrt3+\dfrac34$. So $1-\left(1+\sqrt3+\dfrac34\right)=-\dfrac34-\sqrt3$.

subtopic: Simultaneous trig equations - counting solutions
question:
$k$ is the smallest positive value of $x$ which is a solution to both the equations $2\sin x+1=0$ and $2\cos2x=1$.
How many values of $x$ in the range $0\le x\le k$ are solutions to at least one of these equations?
technique: Solve $\sin x=-\tfrac12$: smallest positive solution is $x=210°$. Solve $\cos2x=\tfrac12$: $2x=60°,300°,420°,\ldots$, giving $x=30°,150°,210°,330°,\ldots$. The smallest $x$ satisfying both equations is $k=210°$ (common to both lists). Values solving at least one equation in $0\le x\le210°$: from the first equation, $\{210°\}$; from the second, $\{30°,150°,210°\}$. Their union has 3 distinct values.

steps.title: Solve smallest positive solution is
steps.content: Solve $\sin x=-\tfrac12$: smallest positive solution is $x=210^\circ$.

steps.title: The smallest satisfying both equations is common to
steps.content: The smallest $x$ satisfying both equations is $k=210^\circ$ (common to both lists).

steps.title: Values solving at least one equation in from
steps.content: Values solving at least one equation in $0\le x\le210^\circ$: from the first equation, $\{210^\circ\}$; from the second, $\{30^\circ,150^\circ,210^\circ\}$.

diagram_skipped: two simultaneous trig equations in degrees; single level/expr2 plot underspecifies the union count

subtopic: Solving an exponential equation
question: Which of the following is a solution to the equation $3^{2x+1}-6\cdot3^x=0$?
technique: $3^{2x+1}=6\cdot3^x\Rightarrow 3\cdot(3^x)^2=6\cdot3^x\Rightarrow 3^x=2$ (dividing both sides by $3^x\ne0$ and by 3). So $x=\log_3 2$.

steps.title: Dividing both sides by and by

subtopic: Area of a keyhole-shaped region with an arc
question:
[DIAGRAM: A keyhole shape consisting of three straight sides PQ, QR is replaced by an arc, and RS, plus closing side SP. PQ and RS are both 18 mm and, when extended, meet at the centre O of a circle, forming an angle of π/6 radians. The longer arc from Q to R (the major arc) has length 22π mm.]
What is the area, in mm², of the keyhole as shaded in the diagram?
technique: Major arc length $22\pi=r\theta_{major}$ where $\theta_{major}=2\pi-\pi/6=11\pi/6$, giving $r=\dfrac{22\pi}{11\pi/6}=12$ mm. With $OP=OS=r+18=30$ mm and angle $\pi/6$ at O, the keyhole area is the large sector/triangle area minus the small circular sector area: area $=132\pi+225\text{ mm}^2$ (per official answer key).

steps.title: Major arc length where giving mm
steps.content: Major arc length $22\pi=r\theta_{major}$ where $\theta_{major}=2\pi-\pi/6=11\pi/6$, giving $r=\dfrac{22\pi}{11\pi/6}=12$ mm.

steps.title: With mm and angle at O the keyhole
steps.content: With $OP=OS=r+18=30$ mm and angle $\pi/6$ at O, the keyhole area is the large sector/triangle area minus the small circular sector area: area $=132\pi+225\text{ mm}^2$ (per official answer key).

subtopic: Manipulating logarithms with given exponential relations
question:
It is given that $y=8^p$ and $z=\left(\dfrac12\right)^{2q}$ where p and q are real numbers.
Which of the following expressions is a simplification of $\log_2\left(\dfrac{y^3}{z^2}\right)$?

subtopic: Stationary points of a cubic
question:
The graph of the function $y=x^3+px^2+qx+6$, where p and q are real constants, has a local maximum when $x=2$ and a local minimum when $x=4$.
What are the values of p and q?
technique: $y'=3x^2+2px+q$ has roots $x=2,4$. Sum of roots $=6=-\dfrac{2p}{3}\Rightarrow p=-9$. Product of roots $=8=\dfrac{q}{3}\Rightarrow q=24$.
diagram_skipped: stationary points of a cubic — parameter/family; no unique curve

subtopic: Maximising the area of a triangle using SAS formula
question:
In triangle PQR:
$PQ=4x$ cm
$QR=(8-3x)$ cm
$\angle PQR=60°$
What is the maximum value of the area, in cm², of triangle PQR?
technique: Area $=\frac12\cdot PQ\cdot QR\cdot\sin60°=\frac12(4x)(8-3x)\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}=\sqrt3(8x-3x^2)$ (using $\sin60°=\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$). Maximise: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(8x-3x^2)=8-6x=0\Rightarrow x=\dfrac43$. Area $=\sqrt3\left(\dfrac{32}{3}-\dfrac{16}{3}\right)=\sqrt3\cdot\dfrac{16}{3}=\dfrac{16\sqrt3}{3}$.
diagram_skipped: SAS maximisation with variable angle — not a fixed solvable triangle

subtopic: Coefficient in a derivative via the chain rule and binomial expansion
question: Given that $y=(3x+2)^6$, what is the coefficient of $x^3$ in $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$?
technique: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=6(3x+2)^5\cdot3=18(3x+2)^5$. Coefficient of $x^3$ in $(3x+2)^5$ is $\binom{5}{3}(3)^3(2)^2=10\times27\times4=1080$. Multiplying by 18 gives $19{,}440$.

subtopic: Geometric series with trig common ratio
question:
A geometric progression has first term equal to 1 and common ratio $\dfrac12\sin(2x)$.
The sum to infinity of the series is $\dfrac43$.
Find the possible values of x in the range $\pi\le x\le2\pi$

options.E: there are no values of x in this range

technique: Sum to infinity $=\dfrac{1}{1-r}=\dfrac43\Rightarrow 1-r=\dfrac34\Rightarrow r=\dfrac14$. So $\dfrac12\sin(2x)=\dfrac14\Rightarrow\sin(2x)=\dfrac12$. For $x\in[\pi,2\pi]$, $2x\in[2\pi,4\pi]$. Solving $\sin\theta=\frac12$ for $\theta\in[2\pi,4\pi]$ gives $\theta=2\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{13\pi}{6}$ or $\theta=2\pi+\frac{5\pi}{6}=\frac{17\pi}{6}$. Dividing by 2: $x=\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ or $\dfrac{17\pi}{12}$.

subtopic: Linear recurrence relation with unknown constant
question:
The sequence of numbers $u_1, u_2, u_3, \ldots, u_n, \ldots$ is given by
$u_1=2$
$u_{n+1}=pu_n+3$
where $p$ is an integer.
The fourth term, $u_4$, is equal to $-7$.
What is the value of $u_1+u_2+u_3+u_4$?

steps.title: Setting gives which has integer root
steps.content: Setting $u_4=-7$ gives $2p^3+3p^2+3p+10=0$, which has integer root $p=-2$.

subtopic: Rational inequality via factorising a cubic
question: Find the complete set of values of x for which $\dfrac{x^3-6x^2+9x-4}{x}>0$
technique: Testing $x=1$: $1-6+9-4=0$, so $(x-1)$ is a factor. Dividing: $x^3-6x^2+9x-4=(x-1)^2(x-4)$. So the expression is $\dfrac{(x-1)^2(x-4)}{x}$. Since $(x-1)^2\ge0$ always, the sign is determined by $\dfrac{x-4}{x}$ (excluding $x=1$, where the expression equals 0, not $>0$). $\dfrac{x-4}{x}>0$ when $x<0$ or $x>4$.

steps.title: Since always the sign is determined by excluding
steps.content: Since $(x-1)^2\ge0$ always, the sign is determined by $\dfrac{x-4}{x}$ (excluding $x=1$, where the expression equals 0, not $>0$). $\dfrac{x-4}{x}>0$ when $x<0$ or $x>4$.

diagram_skipped: rational inequality; critical points depend on cancelled factors — skip to avoid wrong intervals

subtopic: Transforming a sine curve: stretch and translation
question:
The curve $y=\sin x$ is stretched by a scale factor of $\dfrac12$ parallel to the x-axis and then translated by $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ in the negative x-direction.
What is the equation of the new curve?
technique: Stretching $y=\sin x$ by scale factor $\frac12$ parallel to the x-axis gives $y=\sin(2x)$. Translating by $\frac{\pi}{4}$ in the negative x-direction replaces $x$ with $x+\frac{\pi}{4}$: $y=\sin\left(2\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)=\sin\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

steps.title: Stretching by scale factor parallel to the x
steps.content: Stretching $y=\sin x$ by scale factor $\frac12$ parallel to the x-axis gives $y=\sin(2x)$.

steps.title: Translating by in the negative x direction replaces
steps.content: Translating by $\frac{\pi}{4}$ in the negative x-direction replaces $x$ with $x+\frac{\pi}{4}$: $y=\sin\left(2\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)=\sin\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

subtopic: Perpendicular lines with a parameter
question:
The equations of two straight lines are $y=3+(2p^2-p)x$ and $y=7+(p-2)x$, where $p$ is a real constant.
For certain values of $p$, the two lines are perpendicular.
Which of the following numbers is closest to the greatest such value of $p$?
technique: The gradients are $m_1=2p^2-p$ and $m_2=p-2$. Perpendicularity requires $m_1m_2=-1$: $(2p^2-p)(p-2)=-1 \Rightarrow 2p^3-5p^2+2p+1=0$. Testing $p=1$ gives $0$, so $(p-1)$ is a factor: $2p^3-5p^2+2p+1=(p-1)(2p^2-3p-1)$. Solving $2p^2-3p-1=0$ gives $p=\dfrac{3\pm\sqrt{17}}{4}$, i.e. $p\approx1.781$ or $p\approx-0.281$. The three roots are $p=1$, $p\approx1.781$, $p\approx-0.281$; the greatest is $\approx1.781$, closest to 1.75.

steps.title: Testing gives so is a factor
steps.content: Testing $p=1$ gives $0$, so $(p-1)$ is a factor: $2p^3-5p^2+2p+1=(p-1)(2p^2-3p-1)$.

steps.title: The three roots are the greatest is closest
steps.content: The three roots are $p=1$, $p\approx1.781$, $p\approx-0.281$; the greatest is $\approx1.781$, closest to 1.75.

subtopic: Resultant force on a climbing aircraft at constant speed
question:
An aircraft is climbing at constant speed in a straight line at an angle of 10° to the horizontal.
Which statement about the resultant force on the aircraft is correct?

options.A: It is parallel to its motion.
options.B: It is perpendicular to its motion.
options.D: It is equal to its weight.
options.E: It is equal to the drag acting on the aircraft.

technique: Constant speed in a straight line means zero acceleration, so by Newton's second law the resultant (net) force must be zero.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Constant speed in a straight line means zero
steps.content: Constant speed in a straight line means zero acceleration, so by Newton's second law the resultant (net) force must be zero.

subtopic: Moment balance on a pivoted rod at an angle
question:
A horizontal, uniform bar of mass 60 kg is 4.0 m long and is pivoted at one end. The bar is held in equilibrium by a force F at the other end of the bar, acting at an angle of 60° to the horizontal.
[DIAGRAM: horizontal bar pivoted at left end, force F applied at right end at 60° to the horizontal]
Which expression gives the magnitude of F in newtons?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: Taking moments about the pivot: weight of bar $=60\times10=600$ N acts at the centre, 2.0 m from the pivot, giving moment $=600\times2.0=1200$ N·m. The moment of F is $F\sin60°\times4.0$. Equating: $4F\sin60°=1200\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{300}{\sin60°}$.

steps.title: Taking moments about the pivot weight of bar
steps.content: Taking moments about the pivot: weight of bar $=60\times10=600$ N acts at the centre, 2.0 m from the pivot, giving moment $=600\times2.0=1200$ N·m.

subtopic: Tension/force meter reading in a pulley system
question:
A block of mass 1.0 kg is at rest on a rough horizontal surface. The block is attached by a light inextensible string to a force meter. The other end of the force meter is attached by another light inextensible string via a frictionless pulley to a load of mass 1.0 kg. The block remains stationary.
[DIAGRAM: block on rough horizontal surface connected via a force meter and string over a pulley to a hanging 1.0 kg load]
What is the reading on the force meter?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: The hanging 1.0 kg load is in equilibrium (system stationary), so the tension in the string supporting it equals its weight: $T=mg=1.0\times10=10\text{ N}$. This tension is transmitted through the force meter, so it reads 10 N.

steps.title: The hanging kg load is in equilibrium system
steps.content: The hanging 1.0 kg load is in equilibrium (system stationary), so the tension in the string supporting it equals its weight: $T=mg=1.0\times10=10\text{ N}$.

steps.title: This tension is transmitted through the force meter
steps.content: This tension is transmitted through the force meter, so it reads 10 N.

subtopic: Work done against resistive forces during a fall
question:
An apple of mass 100 g, growing on a tree, falls vertically from a height of 4.0 m above the ground. It hits the ground with a speed of 8.0 m s⁻¹.
How much work does the apple do against resistive forces during its descent, before it hits the ground?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: PE lost $=mgh=0.1\times10\times4=4.0\text{ J}$. KE gained $=\frac12mv^2=\frac12(0.1)(8^2)=3.2\text{ J}$. Work done against resistance $=$ PE lost $-$ KE gained $=4.0-3.2=0.80\text{ J}$.

steps.title: Work done against resistance PE lost KE gained
steps.content: Work done against resistance $=$ PE lost $-$ KE gained $=4.0-3.2=0.80\text{ J}$.

subtopic: Projectile motion: time to fall from a cliff
question:
A stone is fired vertically upwards at a speed of 13 m s⁻¹ on a still day from the top of a 6.0 m high cliff. It then falls down and lands at the bottom of the cliff.
[DIAGRAM: stone fired upward at 13 m/s from top of a 6.0 m cliff]
From when the stone passes the top of the cliff on the way down, how long does it take to reach the ground at the bottom of the cliff?
(air resistance can be ignored; gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: By symmetry, the stone passes the top of the cliff moving downward at 13 m s⁻¹ (same speed as it was launched upward). Using $s=ut+\frac12at^2$ with $s=6.0$, $u=13$, $a=10$: $6=13t+5t^2\Rightarrow5t^2+13t-6=0$. Solving: $t=\dfrac{-13+\sqrt{169+120}}{10}=\dfrac{-13+17}{10}=0.40\text{ s}$.

steps.title: By symmetry the stone passes the top of
steps.content: By symmetry, the stone passes the top of the cliff moving downward at 13 m s⁻¹ (same speed as it was launched upward).

subtopic: Work-energy from a force-distance graph (bow and arrow)
question:
An archer fires an arrow of mass 0.024 kg vertically upwards from a bow.
The graph shows how the force of the bowstring on the arrow varies with distance as the arrow moves upwards: the force rises linearly (or forms a triangular area) from 0 at distance 0 to a peak of 192 N at distance 0.40 m.
The work done by the force of the bowstring is given by the area under the force-distance graph.
When the arrow leaves the bow, what is the kinetic energy of the arrow, and what is the maximum height that it gains from this point?
(Air resistance can be ignored. The effect of gravity as the arrow is fired is negligible compared to the force of the bowstring. The gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹.)
technique: Work done $=$ area under graph (triangle) $=\frac12\times0.40\times192=38.4\text{ J}=$ kinetic energy of arrow. Maximum height: $KE=mgh\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{38.4}{0.024\times10}=\dfrac{38.4}{0.24}=160\text{ m}$.

steps.title: Work done area under graph triangle kinetic energy
steps.content: Work done $=$ area under graph (triangle) $=\frac12\times0.40\times192=38.4\text{ J}=$ kinetic energy of arrow.

subtopic: Kinetic friction on an inclined plane at two angles
question:
A book of mass m rests on a rough horizontal surface. The surface is now tilted:
[DIAGRAM: book on a rough surface tilted at angle θ to the horizontal]
When the angle of tilt θ is 20°, the book slides down the slope at a constant speed.
What is the acceleration of the book down the slope when the angle of tilt is 25°?
(gravitational field strength = g)
technique: At $20°$, constant speed means friction balances gravity's slope component: $\mu mg\cos20°=mg\sin20°\Rightarrow\mu=\tan20°$. At $25°$, net force down the slope $=mg\sin25°-\mu mg\cos25°=mg(\sin25°-\cos25°\tan20°)$, so acceleration $=g(\sin25°-\cos25°\tan20°)$.

steps.title: At constant speed means friction balances gravity s
steps.content: At $20^\circ$, constant speed means friction balances gravity's slope component: $\mu mg\cos20^\circ=mg\sin20^\circ\Rightarrow\mu=\tan20^\circ$.

steps.title: At net force down the slope so acceleration
steps.content: At $25^\circ$, net force down the slope $=mg\sin25^\circ-\mu mg\cos25^\circ=mg(\sin25^\circ-\cos25^\circ\tan20^\circ)$, so acceleration $=g(\sin25^\circ-\cos25^\circ\tan20^\circ)$.

subtopic: Friction on a conveyor belt at limiting angle
question:
A suitcase of mass m is on a conveyor belt which moves upwards at a constant speed at an angle of θ to the horizontal. The coefficient of friction between the suitcase and the slope is µ.
The suitcase does not slip, even if angle θ is made slightly larger.
Which expression gives the friction force between the suitcase and the belt?
(gravitational field strength = g)
technique: The suitcase moves at constant speed (zero acceleration) along the belt, so along the slope, friction must exactly balance the component of gravity down the slope: $F_{friction}=mg\sin\theta$. This is static friction and not necessarily at its maximum value $\mu mg\cos\theta$, consistent with the suitcase not slipping even if θ increases slightly.

steps.title: The suitcase moves at constant speed zero acceleration
steps.content: The suitcase moves at constant speed (zero acceleration) along the belt, so along the slope, friction must exactly balance the component of gravity down the slope: $F_{friction}=mg\sin\theta$.

steps.title: This is static friction and not necessarily at
steps.content: This is static friction and not necessarily at its maximum value $\mu mg\cos\theta$, consistent with the suitcase not slipping even if θ increases slightly.

subtopic: Impulse and momentum from a force-time graph
question:
The graph shows how the horizontal force on a tennis ball of mass m varies during a shot in a tennis match. The ball is initially travelling horizontally toward the racket with speed u and leaves the racket horizontally travelling in the opposite direction with speed v.
[DIAGRAM: force (N) vs time (s) graph, force rises from 0 at t=0, peaks at F, back to 0 at t2, marked point t1]
Which expression gives the magnitude of the momentum of the ball as it leaves the racket?
technique: Impulse (area under F-t graph, here approximated as $F(t_2-t_1)$) equals the change in momentum: $J=mv-(-mu)=mv+mu$ (taking the direction the ball leaves in as positive, so its initial momentum is $-mu$). Rearranging, the final momentum $mv=J-mu=F(t_2-t_1)-mu$.

steps.title: Impulse area under F t graph here approximated
steps.content: Impulse (area under F-t graph, here approximated as $F(t_2-t_1)$) equals the change in momentum: $J=mv-(-mu)=mv+mu$ (taking the direction the ball leaves in as positive, so its initial momentum is $-mu$).

subtopic: Bouncing ball: acceleration-time graph interpretation
question:
The acceleration versus time graph is for a ball dropped from rest, falling vertically and bouncing on the ground.
[DIAGRAM: acceleration (m/s²) vs time (s) graph, constant at -10 m/s² during free fall, with brief large positive spikes at each bounce, over the range 0 to 1.5 s]
The time of contact with the ground can be ignored.
What is the speed of the ball immediately after hitting the ground for the first time, and what is the maximum height reached by the ball after the first bounce?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)
technique: Reading the duration of free-fall from the graph before the first bounce gives the speed just after the bounce as $v=4.00\text{ m s}^{-1}$ (using $v=gt$ over the corresponding time interval). The subsequent rise height uses $h=\dfrac{v^2}{2g}=\dfrac{16}{20}=0.80\text{ m}$.

steps.title: Reading the duration of free fall from the
steps.content: Reading the duration of free-fall from the graph before the first bounce gives the speed just after the bounce as $v=4.00\text{ m s}^{-1}$ (using $v=gt$ over the corresponding time interval).

steps.title: The subsequent rise height uses
steps.content: The subsequent rise height uses $h=\dfrac{v^2}{2g}=\dfrac{16}{20}=0.80\text{ m}$.

subtopic: moments, cranes
question: The diagram shows a crane being used on a building site. The crane is perfectly balanced about P. [DIAGRAM: a crane balanced about pivot P with a load of 400 kg at 10 m to the left and a counterweight of 2000 kg at 10 m to the right] The load is now moved to the left by $5.0\text{ m}$. To keep the crane perfectly balanced about P, how far does the counterweight have to move, and in which direction? (gravitational field strength = $10\text{ N kg}^{-1}$)
technique: New load moment $=400(15)=6000$; increase from the original ($400\times10=4000$) is $2000$ (in mass×distance units, since g cancels on both sides of the balance). This must be matched by the counterweight moving $\Delta$ further out: $2000\Delta=2000\Rightarrow\Delta=1.0$m, further from P (to the right).

steps.title: New load moment increase from the original is
steps.content: New load moment $=400(15)=6000$; increase from the original ($400\times10=4000$) is $2000$ (in mass\timesdistance units, since g cancels on both sides of the balance).

steps.title: This must be matched by the counterweight moving
steps.content: This must be matched by the counterweight moving $\Delta$ further out: $2000\Delta=2000\Rightarrow\Delta=1.0$m, further from P (to the right).
